content:
NO-GO.

Main defect: Arm 2 is **not genuinely shortcut-immune as written**. Byte-identical scored strings do not eliminate **context-to-string leakage**:
- mere-mention / lexical priming from the prior discourse can bias a model toward the already-mentioned noun phrase and toward the order that best fits discourse continuity;
- the “given” context can smuggle in **syntactic scaffolding** (e.g. active/passive, clefts, topicalization, heavy NP cues, prosody proxies in punctuation/line breaks) that correlates with split order;
- recency and salience of the object NP can favor the order that places it earlier/later independent of givenness.

To close the channel, the GIVEN vs NEW contexts need a **tight authoring constraint**: same discourse genre, same number of clauses, same sentence types, same verb lemma and object noun mention pattern, same punctuation/formatting, same entity salience except for **one controlled prior mention/topical accessibility manipulation**. Ideally the only difference is: one condition contains an antecedent introducing the referent with no extra word-order or topicalization cues; the other substitutes a discourse-neutral intro with equal length and parallel syntax. If you can’t guarantee that, Arm 2 can still be contaminated.

Second defect: the **confirm rule over-weights a single arm**. If Arm 2 is the load-bearing firewall, then requiring CI-LB>0 on both Arm 1 and Arm 2 is okay only if Arm 1 is explicitly ancillary. But the design text still treats Arm 1 as primary human-direction contrast and Arm 2 as the “confirm rests on Arm 2” test. That is muddled. If Arm 2 is the true probe, say so and demote Arm 1 to external triangulation; don’t let a noisy determiner effect become part of confirm logic unless you want a false-negative machine.

Third: as a focal constraint, **object givenness is defensible**, but length is the cleaner first cut. Length is less discourse-engineered and more directly operationalized; givenness is exactly where shortcut leakage is likeliest. If the aim is a cross-construction generalization from dative to particle placement, fine — but then you need a stronger firewall than currently specified. Otherwise length is the better initial probe.

Fourth: the anchor wording is **too loose**. “Human-anchored on the direction” is honest only if you explicitly label Kim/Gries as **external direction corroboration**, not an anchor that legitimizes a gradient. Right now it risks sounding like you have human-level grounding for a soft preference when you only have a sign. Use internal-contrast-only for the operational verdict; cite Kim/Gries as background support for sign selection.

Bottom line: concept is plausible, but the operationalization still allows a spurious positive on Arm 2 and overstates the evidential role of the external anchor.

VOTE: NO-GO
